🦋 Golden Butterfly Portfolio

Comprehensive backtest analysis — US ETFs, EU ETFs, proxies back to 1962

1. Portfolio Overview

What is the Golden Butterfly and why these allocations?

The Golden Butterfly is a permanent-portfolio variant designed by Tyler at portfoliocharts.com. It splits equally across five asset classes, each serving a distinct economic role: stocks for growth, small-cap value for excess return, long bonds for deflation protection, short bonds for stability, and gold for inflation hedging. The equal 20% weighting keeps the portfolio robust across all economic regimes — growth, recession, inflation, and deflation.

20%
Total Stock Market
Broad equity exposure — captures overall market growth
20%
Small Cap Value
Tilts toward small, undervalued companies — historically higher returns
20%
Long-Term Bonds
20+ year Treasuries — strong deflation hedge, inversely correlated with stocks
20%
Short-Term Bonds
1-3 year Treasuries — stability anchor, low volatility cash-like returns
20%
Gold
Inflation hedge and crisis diversifier — uncorrelated to stocks and bonds
📌 Instruments Used
ComponentUS ETFFund ProxyUS Deep Proxy
Total Market ⓘVTI Vanguard Total Stock^GSPC S&P 500^GSPC S&P 500
Small Cap Value ⓘVBR Vanguard SCV^RUT Russell 2000^GSPC+2% Synthetic premium
Long-Term Bonds ⓘTLT 20+yr TreasuryVUSTX LT Treasury FundDGS10 FRED yield → return
Short-Term Bonds ⓘSHY 1-3yr TreasuryVFISX ST Treasury FundDGS1+DGS2 FRED yield → return
Gold ⓘGLD SPDR GoldGC=F Gold FuturesSynthetic Milestones + GC=F
GREEN Real ETF   GOLD Index / Mutual Fund   RED Synthetic / Model-based

1. Portfolio Overview

What is the Golden Butterfly and why these allocations?

The Golden Butterfly is a permanent-portfolio variant designed by Tyler at portfoliocharts.com. It splits equally across five asset classes, each serving a distinct economic role: stocks for growth, small-cap value for excess return, long bonds for deflation protection, short bonds for stability, and gold for inflation hedging. The equal 20% weighting keeps the portfolio robust across all economic regimes — growth, recession, inflation, and deflation.

20%
Total Stock Market
Broad equity exposure — captures overall market growth
20%

Tilts toward small, undervalued companies — historically higher returns
20%

20+ year Treasuries — strong deflation hedge, inversely correlated with stocks

1-3 year Treasuries — stability anchor, low volatility cash-like returns
20%

Inflation hedge and crisis diversifier — uncorrelated to stocks and bonds
📌 Instruments Used
ComponentEU ETFEU Deep Proxy
Total Market ⓘIWDA.AS MSCI World60/20/20 ^GSPC+^N225+^FTSE → EUR
Small Cap Value ⓘIUSN.DE MSCI World SC^RUT/^GSPC+2% → EUR
Long-Term Bonds ⓘDTLA.L $ Treasury 20+yrDGS10 FRED yield → return (USD)
Short-Term Bonds ⓘIBTA.L $ Treasury 1-3yr (USD)DGS1+DGS2 FRED yield → return (USD)
Gold ⓘIGLN.L Physical GoldSynthetic Milestones + GC=F (USD)
Currency ⓘEUR (equity) + USD (bonds, gold)FRED EUR/USD for equity → EUR
GREEN Real ETF   GOLD Index / Mutual Fund   RED Synthetic / Model-based
💱 Currency Exposure Note
This EU portfolio is 60% USD-denominated (bonds + gold) and 40% EUR-denominated (equities). This is intentional: US Treasuries are the deepest, most liquid bond market, gold is globally priced in USD, and the USD exposure itself acts as diversification for a EUR-based investor. In practice, a weakening EUR boosts the USD portion and vice versa — adding an extra layer of uncorrelated return. If you prefer to neutralize FX risk, look for EUR-hedged share classes (e.g. DTLA.L → IDTL.L, IGLN.L → SGLN.L).

3. Cumulative Returns — All Variants

Full History

Log scale · drag to zoom · growth and drawdown are linked

Each variant starts from its own inception date. The log scale makes growth rates visually comparable (parallel lines = same CAGR), but be aware that longer series have more time to compound — the US Proxy’s 104x growth reflects 63 years, not superior returns. Zoom into any period and the drawdown chart below follows automatically.

Loading chart…

Common Period (2004-11-19 → 2026-03-25)

To compare fairly, all four variants are rebased to $1 from the same start date. This is the key validation view: if the US Proxy and Fund Proxy track the real ETF portfolios closely over this shared window, we can trust their behavior in earlier decades where no ETF data exists. Any persistent gap between lines reveals systematic bias in the proxy construction.

4. Proxy vs ETF Validation

Can we trust the proxies? Let's check against real ETF data.

The whole point of building proxies is to extend the backtest beyond ETF inception dates. But a proxy is only useful if it behaves like the real thing when we can compare them. Below, each proxy is tested against each available ETF portfolio over their exact overlapping period. Four tests per pair:

📈 Cumulative growth — do they arrive at the same destination?
🔵 Monthly scatter + R² — is the month-to-month relationship tight?
📊 Rolling correlation — is tracking consistent or does it break down?
📉 Tracking error — how much daily divergence exists?
How to read the verdicts
PASS = proxy reliably tracks the ETF   MARGINAL = usable for broad conclusions, not precise attribution   FAIL = proxy diverges significantly

US ETF vs US Proxy

Overlapping period: 2004-11-19 → 2026-03-25 (21.1 years). All statistics use weekly returns to filter out daily noise and timezone mismatches. If US Proxy is a good stand-in for US ETF, the lines below should overlap, the scatter should hug the diagonal, and correlation should stay above 0.85.

Cumulative Growth — do they end at the same place?

Loading chart…

Weekly Scatter — R² measures how predictable the relationship is

Loading chart…

Rolling 52-Week Correlation — stability of the relationship over time

Loading chart…

Rolling 52-Week Tracking Error — annualized divergence (lower = better)

Total Market^GSPC (S&P 500)0.98Excellent. S&P 500 covers ~80% of US market cap, very close to VTI.

GoldHistorical milestones + GC=F0.88Good. Pre-2000 gold uses interpolated annual prices (smoothed), post-2000 uses actual futures.

Can we trust the proxies? Let's check against real ETF data.

The whole point of building proxies is to extend the backtest beyond ETF inception dates. But a proxy is only useful if it behaves like the real thing when we can compare them. Below, each proxy is tested against each available ETF portfolio over their exact overlapping period. Four tests per pair:

7. Per-Decade Breakdown (US Proxy)

How the Golden Butterfly performed across different economic regimes

The deep proxy lets us examine performance across seven decades, each with distinct macro conditions: the inflationary 1970s, the bond bull market of the 1980s-90s, the dot-com crash and GFC in the 2000s, and the post-QE 2010s.

Loading chart…
🎯 Key Observations
• Best decade: 1980s with 10.0% CAGR
• Worst decade: 1960s with 2.3% CAGR — still positive
• The portfolio never had a lost decade — every 10-year period delivered positive real returns
• Max drawdown stayed between -21.3% and -7.6% across all decades
• The 1970s (high inflation) had the third-best CAGR thanks to the gold allocation

8. Conclusions — US Analysis

What 63 years of data tell us about the Golden Butterfly

🦋 The Golden Butterfly in Numbers (1962–2026, US Proxy)

CAGR 7.6% Volatility 7.7% Max DD -21.3% Sharpe 0.6

1. Consistent real returns across all regimes. The portfolio delivered positive returns in every single decade since 1962, including the inflationary 1970s (9.2% CAGR thanks to gold) and the brutal 2000s (5.6% CAGR while the S&P 500 was flat). No lost decade.

2. Drawdowns are contained. Max drawdown never exceeded -21.3% in 63 years — roughly half of what a pure equity portfolio experiences. The longest underwater period was ~661 trading days (~2.6 years), vs 5+ years for stocks alone.

3. The proxy is reliable but imperfect. With 0.9323 daily portfolio-level correlation (0.95 weekly) and ~2.7% weekly tracking error, the deep proxy is good enough for strategic conclusions but not for precise return attribution.

4. The cost of safety. The Golden Butterfly's ~7.6% CAGR trails a 100% equity portfolio (~10% CAGR) by about 2.5% annually. The tradeoff: roughly half the drawdown, much shorter recovery times, and a smoother ride that most investors can actually stick with.

⚠️ Limitations
• Pre-2000 gold uses interpolated annual prices (no daily volatility captured)
• SCV proxy is S&P 500 + flat premium (misses actual small-value factor dynamics)
• Bond proxies use duration models, not actual bond returns (convexity simplified)
• No transaction costs, taxes, or rebalancing friction included

8. Conclusions — EU Analysis

What the data tells an EU-based investor

🦋 EU Golden Butterfly — Key Numbers

EU ETF (7.8yr)
CAGR 8.0% Vol 8.2% Max DD -16.8%
EU Proxy (63.4yr)
CAGR 6.8% Vol 7.1% Max DD -17.8%

1. The strategy works in EUR. Over the common period (2018–2026), the EU ETF version delivered 8.0% CAGR with 8.2% volatility — comparable to the US ETF (8.1% CAGR). The broader geographic diversification (MSCI World vs US-only) results in slightly lower volatility.

2. Currency is a feature, not a bug. 60% of the portfolio (bonds + gold) is USD-denominated. This is intentional: USD exposure acts as diversification for a EUR investor. During EUR weakness, the USD portion boosts returns. Over full cycles, the FX effect tends to wash out, but it adds short-term volatility.

3. The EU proxy is usable but less precise. Weekly correlation of 0.92 and 3.4% tracking error reflect the difficulty of modeling MSCI World returns with a three-index blend (^GSPC + ^N225 + ^FTSE) and imperfect FX conversion. The proxy is reliable enough for strategic conclusions about the strategy's behavior across decades, but not for precise return attribution.

4. Short history is the main limitation. The EU ETF backtest covers only ~7.8 years — too short to draw definitive conclusions about tail risk or decade-level consistency. The EU proxy extends this to 63.4 years, but with lower fidelity. For high-confidence analysis, refer to the US results.

⚠️ EU-Specific Limitations
• IUSN.DE tracks MSCI World Small Cap (all small caps), not specifically Small Cap Value
• EU proxy equity blend (60/20/20) is a rough approximation of MSCI World composition
• FX conversion uses daily FRED EUR/USD rates, not real-time market rates
• Pre-1999 proxy has no EUR conversion (EUR didn't exist)
• No transaction costs, withholding taxes, or currency conversion fees included

9. Data Sources, Methodology & Notes

Full transparency on where the data comes from and how proxies are built

📡 Data Sources

SourceWhatCoverageNotes
Yahoo FinanceETF & index prices (VTI, VBR, TLT, SHY, GLD, IWDA, IUSN, DTLA, IBTS, IGLN, ^GSPC, ^RUT, GC=F)Varies per tickerAdjusted close prices, auto-adjusted for splits & dividends
Yahoo FinanceMutual fund NAVs (VUSTX, VFISX)~1986+, ~1991+Vanguard Long-Term & Short-Term Treasury funds
FRED (St. Louis Fed)DGS10 — 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Rate1962-01-02 → presentDaily yield in %, used for LT bond proxy via duration model
FRED (St. Louis Fed)DGS1 — 1-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Rate1962-01-02 → presentDaily yield in %, used for ST bond proxy pre-1976 via duration model
FRED (St. Louis Fed)DGS2 — 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Rate1976-06-01 → presentDaily yield in %, used for ST bond proxy from 1976 via duration model
Historical recordsGold annual average prices (USD/oz)1950–2000Manually compiled milestones from World Gold Council & LBMA records

🏗️ ETF Details

TickerNameRegionRoleData Since

IUSN.DEiShares MSCI World Small Cap UCITS ETFEU (XETRA)Small Cap Value*2018

IBTA.LiShares $ Treasury Bond 1-3yr UCITS ETF (USD)EU (LSE)Short-Term Bonds2017
IGLN.LiShares Physical Gold ETCEU (LSE)Gold2011

* IUSN tracks MSCI World Small Cap (all small caps), not specifically Small Cap Value. A pure SCV EU ETF (e.g. ZPRV for US SCV, ZPRX for EU SCV from SPDR) would be more accurate but has shorter history.

🔧 Synthetic Proxy Methodology

Total Market → S&P 500 (^GSPC) — The S&P 500 covers ~80% of US equity market cap. It excludes mid/small caps that VTI includes, but the correlation is >0.98. Available from 1950.
Small Cap Value → S&P 500 + 2% annual premium — In the absence of SCV index data before 1987, we apply a constant 2% annualized premium to S&P 500 daily returns. This is based on the Fama-French historical size+value premium (~2-3% annually). The limitation: real SCV has higher volatility and different drawdown timing, so this proxy understates risk and smooths returns. Correlation with VBR in the overlap period: 0.89.
Long-Term Bonds → FRED DGS10 yield with duration model (d=14) — We convert the daily 10-Year Treasury yield into a total return index using the formula:
daily_return = yield/252 − duration × Δyield / (1 + yield)
The first term is coupon income, the second is price change via modified duration. We use duration=14 (higher than the 10Y bond's ~8) to approximate 20+yr bond behavior. Daily yield changes are clamped to ±2% to avoid spikes from data gaps. Correlation with TLT: 0.90. The model simplifies convexity (the non-linear price/yield relationship).
Short-Term Bonds → FRED DGS1 (pre-1976) + DGS2 (1976+) with duration model (d=1.9) — We use the 2-Year Treasury yield (DGS2) where available, falling back to the 1-Year yield (DGS1) before 1976. Duration=1.9 matches SHY’s effective duration (~1.9 years). This is a major improvement over the previous DTB3 (3-month T-bill) proxy: correlation with SHY jumps from 0.29 to 0.96, and tracking error drops from 1.3% to 0.4%. The remaining gap comes from the model’s simplified convexity and the fact that SHY holds a ladder of bonds, not a single 2-year issue.
Gold → Historical milestones (1950-2000) + GC=F futures (2000+) — Pre-1971 (Bretton Woods), gold was fixed at $35/oz. From 1968-2000, we use known annual average prices from World Gold Council records, interpolated daily with time-weighted interpolation. This means pre-2000 gold has no daily volatility — it's a smooth curve. Post-2000, actual gold futures (GC=F) are used. The series is spliced by scaling the milestone series to match the first GC=F price. Correlation with GLD in overlap: 0.88.

⚙️ Portfolio Construction

Allocation: Equal 20% to each of the five components. Rebalancing is implicit (daily returns are weighted, equivalent to daily rebalancing). In practice, annual or threshold-based rebalancing would produce slightly different results.
Risk-free rate: 3% annualized, used for Sharpe and Sortino ratio calculations. This is a simplification — the actual risk-free rate varied from ~0% (2010s) to ~15% (early 1980s).
Costs not included: Expense ratios (typically 0.03-0.20% for these ETFs), trading commissions, bid-ask spreads, rebalancing costs, taxes on dividends/capital gains, and currency conversion costs (relevant for EU investors holding USD-denominated assets).
Dividends: Yahoo Finance adjusted close prices include reinvested dividends for ETFs. The FRED yield-based proxies implicitly include coupon income via the yield term in the model. The gold series does not pay dividends (gold has no yield).

10. Metric Glossary

Click any metric to expand its definition, formula, and interpretation

Returns
Total Return
(1 + r₁)(1 + r₂)…(1 + rₙ) − 1
What: The raw cumulative gain over the entire period. A $1 investment growing to $5.24 means 424% total return.
Why it matters: Gives the big picture, but misleading for comparing different time spans — a 10-year and 30-year backtest aren't comparable on total return alone.
CAGR
(Ending Value / Starting Value)^(1/years) − 1
What: The constant annual rate that, compounded over the period, reproduces the total return. Smooths out year-to-year noise into a single annualized number.
Why it matters: The most important single metric for comparing strategies across different time periods. A 7.6% CAGR means your money doubles roughly every 0.9 years.
Best / Worst 1Y
max / min of rolling 252-day cumulative return
What: The best and worst rolling 12-month windows in the backtest. Shows the range of outcomes an investor might experience in any given year.
Why it matters: Helps set expectations. If the worst year was −20%, you should be psychologically prepared for that. If the best was +35%, don't expect it every year.
Daily Win Rate
count(daily return > 0) / total days
What: Percentage of trading days that ended positive. Typically 53–55% for diversified portfolios.
Why it matters: A sanity check. Much below 50% suggests structural drag; much above 55% is unusual and may indicate smoothed proxy data.
Risk
Volatility
σ_daily × √252
What: Annualized standard deviation of daily returns. Measures how much the portfolio value fluctuates day to day.
Why it matters: The most common risk measure. Lower volatility means a smoother ride. The Golden Butterfly runs at ~7.7% vol (US Proxy) to ~9.1% (US ETF) vs ~16% for pure equities — roughly half the turbulence.
Max Drawdown
min(cumulative / cumulative_max − 1)
What: The largest peak-to-trough decline at any point in the backtest. If you invested at the worst possible moment and sold at the worst possible moment, this is your loss.
Why it matters: The metric that makes or breaks investor behavior. Most people abandon strategies after drawdowns exceeding their pain threshold. A −21% max DD is much more survivable than −55%.
Longest Drawdown
max consecutive days where portfolio < previous high
What: How many trading days the portfolio stayed underwater (below its all-time high). Measures recovery time.
Why it matters: Even more psychologically important than max DD depth. Being underwater for 661 days (~2.6 years) is tough; for pure equities it can exceed 5+ years.
VaR 95%
5th percentile of daily returns
What: Value at Risk: on 95% of trading days, the daily loss will not exceed this threshold. A VaR of -0.72% means you can expect to lose more than 0.72% on only 1 in 20 days.
Why it matters: A regulatory standard for risk measurement. Useful for sizing positions and understanding daily tail exposure, but doesn't tell you how bad the worst days get.
CVaR 95%
mean(daily returns where return ≤ VaR_95)
What: Conditional VaR (Expected Shortfall): the average loss on the worst 5% of days. Goes beyond VaR by answering 'when things go bad, how bad do they get?'
Why it matters: Preferred over VaR by risk professionals because it captures tail severity. A CVaR of -1.09% means that on the worst 1-in-20 days, you lose 1.09% on average.
Ulcer Index
√(mean(drawdown²))
What: Root mean square of all drawdowns over time. Unlike max drawdown (a single worst point), this captures both the depth and duration of every drawdown.
Why it matters: Invented by Peter Martin. A more holistic pain measure — a portfolio with frequent shallow drawdowns and one with rare deep ones can have the same max DD but very different Ulcer Indices. Lower = less stressful to hold.
Risk-Adjusted Returns
Sharpe Ratio
(R_portfolio − R_riskfree) / σ_portfolio
What: Excess return per unit of total risk. Answers: 'how much return am I getting for each unit of volatility I'm enduring?' Uses 3% annual risk-free rate.
Why it matters: The gold standard for risk-adjusted comparison. Below 0.3 is poor, 0.5–0.7 is decent, above 1.0 is excellent. The Golden Butterfly's ~0.6 Sharpe is solid for a multi-asset portfolio.
Sortino Ratio
(R_portfolio − R_riskfree) / σ_downside
What: Like Sharpe, but only penalizes downside volatility (negative returns). Upside volatility is good — you don't want to be penalized for big gains.
Why it matters: More appropriate than Sharpe for portfolios with asymmetric returns. A Sortino of 0.81 vs Sharpe of 0.6 tells you the portfolio's upside volatility is helping, not hurting.
Calmar Ratio
CAGR / |Max Drawdown|
What: Return per unit of worst-case drawdown. Directly links your annual return to the worst pain you'd endure.
Why it matters: Practical for setting expectations: a Calmar of 0.36 means you earn ~0.36% of annual return for every 1% of max drawdown risk. Higher is better — you want more return per unit of worst-case pain.
Distribution Shape
Skewness
E[(r − μ)³] / σ³
What: Measures asymmetry of the return distribution. Zero = symmetric. Negative = fatter left tail (more extreme losses than gains). Positive = fatter right tail.
Why it matters: Most equity portfolios have negative skew (crashes are sharper than rallies). The Golden Butterfly's skew of -0.291 is mild — gold and bonds partially offset equity crash risk.
Kurtosis
E[(r − μ)⁴] / σ⁴ − 3
What: Excess kurtosis measures how fat the tails are compared to a normal distribution. Zero = normal. Positive = more extreme events than a bell curve predicts.
Why it matters: A kurtosis of 11 means extreme days (both good and bad) happen far more often than a normal distribution would suggest. This is why VaR alone is insufficient — the tails are fat.
Generated from Yahoo Finance & FRED data. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
